title: GDPR Data Minimisation Compliance in Logs and APIs

app: Splunk Edge Processor (Splunk Cloud Platform), HTTP Event Collector (HEC)
spl:
index=app sourcetype="api:json" earliest=-24h
| spath path=body{}
| fields _raw endpoint purpose
| rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?<field>\"(ssn|dob|health|biometric)[^\"]*\")"
| lookup gdpr_field_classification.csv field_name OUTPUT sensitivity, allowed_purposes
| where sensitivity="special_category" AND NOT match(allowed_purposes, purpose)
| stats count by endpoint, purpose, field_name
| sort - count
description: Data minimisation requires limiting collection to what is adequate, relevant, and limited. This use case detects API and application log fields classified as excessive for the documented purpose — for example special-category fields in general application logs — using schema metadata and sampling.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for GDPR compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes app and sourcetypes api:json. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Build `gdpr_field_classification.csv` from data dictionary; (2) tune `spath`/`rex` to your JSON shapes; (3) alert API owners on minimisation violations; (4) route critical patterns to Edge Processor drop rules; (5) feed results into DPIA updates. Indexes required: `index=app`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=api:json`.
visualization: Table (violating endpoints), Bar chart (field frequency), Single value (daily violation events), Pie chart (by sensitivity class).
knownFalsePositives: False positives are usually explained by an approved process change, training activity, or time-bound exception recorded in the GRC or change system.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Edge Processor (Splunk Cloud Platform), HTTP Event Collector (HEC).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=app` (API request logs), `gdpr_field_classification.csv` (field_name, sensitivity, allowed_purposes).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Build `gdpr_field_classification.csv` from data dictionary; (2) tune `spath`/`rex` to your JSON shapes; (3) alert API owners on minimisation violations; (4) route critical patterns to Edge Processor drop rules; (5) feed results into DPIA updates.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=app sourcetype="api:json" earliest=-24h
| spath path=body{}
| fields _raw endpoint purpose
| rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?<field>\"(ssn|dob|health|biometric)[^\"]*\")"
| lookup gdpr_field_classification.csv field_name OUTPUT sensitivity, allowed_purposes
| where sensitivity="special_category" AND NOT match(allowed_purposes, purpose)
| stats count by endpoint, purpose, field_name
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Data Minimisation Compliance in Logs and APIs (Art. 25(2), Art. 5(1)(c))** — Data minimisation requires limiting collection to what is adequate, relevant, and limited. This use case detects API and application log fields classified as excessive for the documented purpose — for example special-category fields in general application logs — using schema metadata and sampling.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=app` (API request logs), `gdpr_field_classification.csv` (field_name, sensitivity, allowed_purposes). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Edge Processor (Splunk Cloud Platform), HTTP Event Collector (HEC). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: app; **sourcetype**: api:json. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=app, sourcetype="api:json", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Extracts structured paths (JSON/XML) with `spath`.
• Keeps or drops fields with `fields` to shape columns and size.
• Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where sensitivity="special_category" AND NOT match(allowed_purposes, purpose)` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by endpoint, purpose, field_name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (violating endpoints), Bar chart (field frequency), Single value (daily violation events), Pie chart (by sensitivity class).
grandmaExplanation: We data minimisation requires limiting collection to what is adequate, relevant, and limited so we can show we meet our privacy duties when people ask for proof.
